skills.teks_description: Predict electric force magnitude between objects using Coulomb's law
skills.prompt: This topic covers electrostatics and electric fields for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.6(A)). Students should analyze electric forces, fields, and potential in complex charge configurations. Coulomb's law: F = kq1q2/r², where k = 8.99 × 10^9 N·m²/C². Electric field: E = F/q = kQ/r² for point charges; field is a vector that represents force per unit positive charge. Field lines: originate on positive charges, terminate on negative charges; line density represents field strength; lines never cross. Superposition: net field at a point is the vector sum of fields from all charges. Gauss's law: ΦE = Q_enclosed/ε0; electric flux through a closed surface proportional to enclosed charge; useful for symmetric charge distributions (spherical, cylindrical, planar). Conductors in electrostatic equilibrium: E = 0 inside, excess charge on surface, field perpendicular to surface, strongest at points of highest curvature. Electric dipoles: pair of equal and opposite charges; dipole moment p = qd; experience torque in uniform fields and net force in nonuniform fields. Vocabulary: electric field, field lines, flux, Gauss's law, conductor, insulator, dipole, polarization, induced charge, electrostatic equilibrium, permittivity. Common misconceptions: electric field exists only between charges; field lines represent paths of moving charges.

skills.teks_description: Calculate current, potential difference, resistance, and power using Ohm's law
skills.prompt: This topic covers electric potential and capacitance for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.6(E)). Students should understand voltage, energy storage, and capacitor behavior. Electric potential: V = kQ/r for point charge; V = U/q (potential energy per unit charge); measured in volts (J/C). Potential difference: ΔV = -∫E·dr; work done per unit charge moving between two points; equipotential surfaces are perpendicular to field lines. Electric potential energy: U = kq1q2/r for point charges; U = qV. Capacitance: C = Q/V, ability to store charge; measured in farads (F). Parallel plate capacitor: C = ε0A/d (larger area and smaller separation increase capacitance). Energy stored: U = ½CV² = ½QV = Q²/(2C). Dielectrics: insulating materials between plates increase capacitance by factor κ (dielectric constant); C = κε0A/d. Capacitors in parallel: Ceq = C1 + C2 (same voltage, charges add). Capacitors in series: 1/Ceq = 1/C1 + 1/C2 (same charge, voltages add). Key concepts: relationship between E and V, E = -dV/dx, connecting circuits to field concepts. Vocabulary: electric potential, voltage, equipotential, capacitance, capacitor, dielectric, farad, energy density, parallel plate. Common misconceptions: voltage and current are the same; capacitors store current; larger capacitors always store more energy.

skills.teks_description: Identify electric and magnetic forces and fields in everyday applications
skills.prompt: This topic covers magnetic fields for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.6(B)). Students should understand magnetic field sources, forces on moving charges and currents, and applications. Magnetic field sources: moving charges, current-carrying wires, permanent magnets. Field from long straight wire: B = μ0I/(2πr), where μ0 = 4π × 10^-7 T·m/A. Field inside solenoid: B = μ0nI (uniform). Force on moving charge: F = qvB sin(θ), perpendicular to both v and B (right-hand rule); charged particles follow circular or helical paths in uniform fields with radius r = mv/(qB). Force on current-carrying wire: F = BIL sin(θ). Torque on current loop: τ = NIAB sin(θ); basis for electric motors. Ampere's law: ∮B·dl = μ0I_enclosed; useful for finding B with high symmetry. Magnetic materials: ferromagnetic (iron, domains align), paramagnetic (weakly attracted), diamagnetic (weakly repelled). Key concepts: right-hand rules for field direction and force direction, mass spectrometer operation, velocity selector (crossed E and B fields), Hall effect. Vocabulary: magnetic field, tesla, permeability, right-hand rule, solenoid, torque, domain, ferromagnetic, paramagnetic, diamagnetic, Ampere's law, Hall effect. Common misconceptions: magnetic force does work on charged particles (it changes direction only); all metals are magnetic.

skills.teks_description: Identify electromagnetic induction in generators, motors, and transformers
skills.prompt: This topic covers electromagnetic induction for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.6(B)). Students should understand Faraday's law, Lenz's law, and applications of electromagnetic induction. Magnetic flux: Φ = BA cos(θ), measured in webers (Wb). Faraday's law: induced emf = -NΔΦ/Δt; a changing magnetic flux induces an electromotive force. Ways to change flux: change B, change A, change θ, or move the loop relative to the field. Lenz's law: induced current flows in a direction to oppose the change in flux that caused it (conservation of energy). Motional emf: emf = BLv for a conductor of length L moving with velocity v perpendicular to field B. Applications: AC generators (rotating coil in magnetic field produces sinusoidal emf), transformers (Vs/Vp = Ns/Np; step-up increases voltage, step-down decreases), eddy currents (induced in bulk conductors, used for braking and metal detection). Self-inductance: L = NΦ/I; emf = -L(dI/dt); inductor opposes changes in current. Energy stored in inductor: U = ½LI². LC circuits: oscillation between electric and magnetic energy. Vocabulary: magnetic flux, Faraday's law, Lenz's law, induced emf, generator, transformer, eddy current, inductance, inductor, motional emf, weber. Common misconceptions: a static magnetic field induces current; transformers work with DC; induced current flows in the same direction as the change in flux.

skills.teks_description: Analyze and construct circuits using schematics including AC circuit analysis
skills.prompt: This topic covers AC circuits for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.6(D)). Students should analyze alternating current circuits with resistors, capacitors, and inductors. AC fundamentals: voltage varies sinusoidally V(t) = V0 sin(ωt); current also varies sinusoidally; frequency f = ω/(2π); typical household AC is 120 V RMS at 60 Hz. RMS values: Vrms = V0/√2, Irms = I0/√2; represent the equivalent DC values for power calculations. Impedance components: resistance R (voltage in phase with current), capacitive reactance XC = 1/(ωC) (current leads voltage by 90°), inductive reactance XL = ωL (current lags voltage by 90°). Impedance: Z = √(R² + (XL - XC)²); V = IZ. Phase angle: tan(φ) = (XL - XC)/R. Power: P = IrmsVrms cos(φ), where cos(φ) is the power factor. RLC series circuit: resonance occurs when XL = XC (impedance is minimum, current is maximum); resonant frequency f0 = 1/(2π√(LC)). Transformers: Vs/Vp = Ns/Np = Ip/Is; power is conserved (ideally). Key concepts: phasor diagrams, frequency response, bandwidth, quality factor. Vocabulary: alternating current, RMS, impedance, reactance, resonance, phasor, power factor, transformer, step-up, step-down, bandwidth, quality factor. Common misconceptions: AC and DC behave the same in circuits; capacitors and inductors dissipate power.

skills.teks_description: Compare transverse and longitudinal waves including sound waves
skills.prompt: This topic covers mechanical waves and sound for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.8(B)). Students should analyze wave behavior in depth, including standing waves and the Doppler effect. Wave fundamentals: mechanical waves require a medium; transverse (string, surface) vs. longitudinal (sound, spring compression). Wave equation: v = fλ. Sound: longitudinal pressure wave; speed depends on medium properties (vsound in air ≈ 343 m/s at 20°C, faster in denser media). Sound intensity: I = P/(4πr²) for point source (inverse-square law); intensity level β = 10 log(I/I0) in decibels, where I0 = 10^-12 W/m². Standing waves: superposition of incident and reflected waves. Strings (fixed-fixed): fn = nv/(2L), all harmonics. Open pipe: fn = nv/(2L), all harmonics. Closed pipe (one end closed): fn = nv/(4L), odd harmonics only. Beats: interference of waves with slightly different frequencies; fbeat = |f1 - f2|. Doppler effect: fo = fs(v ± vo)/(v ∓ vs); approaching = higher frequency, receding = lower frequency. Shock waves occur when source exceeds wave speed (Mach cone). Vocabulary: standing wave, harmonic, fundamental, overtone, node, antinode, resonance, beats, Doppler effect, intensity, decibel, Mach number, shock wave. Common misconceptions: sound travels fastest in air; higher harmonics are always louder; Doppler effect changes wave speed.

skills.teks_description: Describe image formation through reflection and refraction
skills.prompt: This topic covers ray and wave optics for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.8(G)). Students should understand image formation through reflection and refraction, and wave optics phenomena. Reflection: law of reflection (θi = θr). Plane mirrors: virtual, upright, same-size images. Curved mirrors: mirror equation 1/f = 1/do + 1/di; magnification M = -di/do; concave mirrors (f > 0) can form real or virtual images; convex mirrors (f < 0) always form virtual images. Refraction: Snell's law n1 sin(θ1) = n2 sin(θ2); total internal reflection when θ > θc = sin^-1(n2/n1). Lenses: thin lens equation 1/f = 1/do + 1/di; convex (converging, f > 0) and concave (diverging, f < 0). Wave optics: double-slit interference d sin(θ) = mλ (maxima); single-slit diffraction a sin(θ) = mλ (minima); thin film interference (phase changes upon reflection from higher-n medium). Diffraction gratings: d sin(θ) = mλ, sharper maxima than double slit. Resolution: Rayleigh criterion θmin = 1.22λ/D. Polarization: Malus's law I = I0 cos²(θ); Brewster's angle. Vocabulary: reflection, refraction, index of refraction, focal length, real image, virtual image, magnification, total internal reflection, diffraction, interference, polarization, Rayleigh criterion. Common misconceptions: lenses only form real images; virtual images cannot be seen; all light bends the same amount in refraction.

skills.teks_description: Describe photoelectric effect and emission spectra explained by photon model
skills.prompt: This topic covers quantum physics for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.9(A)). Students should understand the photon model and quantum phenomena. Photon model: light consists of discrete packets of energy (photons); E = hf = hc/λ, where h = 6.626 × 10^-34 J·s. Photoelectric effect: photons eject electrons from metal surface; KEmax = hf - φ (work function); threshold frequency f0 = φ/h; below f0, no electrons ejected regardless of intensity. Key observations explained by photon model: instantaneous emission above threshold, KE depends on frequency not intensity, number of electrons depends on intensity. Emission spectra: atoms emit photons at discrete frequencies when electrons transition between energy levels; Bohr model: En = -13.6/n² eV for hydrogen. Absorption spectra: atoms absorb photons matching energy level differences. Photon momentum: p = h/λ = hf/c. Compton scattering: photon-electron collision demonstrating particle nature of light. de Broglie wavelength: λ = h/mv; all matter has wave properties, significant only at atomic scale. Vocabulary: photon, photoelectric effect, work function, threshold frequency, emission spectrum, absorption spectrum, Bohr model, energy level, de Broglie wavelength, Compton scattering, wave-particle duality, quantization. Common misconceptions: brighter light always ejects electrons; electrons orbit nuclei like planets; photons have mass.

skills.teks_description: Provide examples of quantum and nuclear phenomena applications
skills.prompt: This topic covers atomic and nuclear physics for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.9(D)). Students should understand nuclear structure, radioactivity, and nuclear reactions. Nuclear structure: nucleus contains protons (Z) and neutrons (N); mass number A = Z + N; isotopes have same Z, different N. Nuclear forces: strong nuclear force (short-range, holds nucleons together, overcomes electromagnetic repulsion), weak nuclear force (responsible for beta decay). Mass-energy equivalence: E = mc²; mass defect Δm = (Zmp + Nmn) - mnucleus; binding energy Eb = Δmc². Binding energy per nucleon: peaks at iron-56 (most stable); explains why fission of heavy nuclei and fusion of light nuclei release energy. Radioactive decay: alpha (⁴₂He emission, Z decreases by 2, A by 4), beta-minus (neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino), beta-plus (proton → neutron + positron + neutrino), gamma (high-energy photon, no change in A or Z). Half-life: N = N0(½)^(t/t½); activity decreases exponentially. Fission: heavy nucleus splits (e.g., U-235 + neutron), chain reaction, nuclear reactors. Fusion: light nuclei combine (e.g., H → He in stars), requires extreme temperatures. Applications: nuclear power, medical imaging (PET, MRI), radiometric dating. Vocabulary: isotope, mass number, binding energy, mass defect, radioactive decay, half-life, alpha, beta, gamma, fission, fusion, chain reaction, activity. Common misconceptions: radioactive materials glow green; all radiation is harmful; fission and fusion are the same.

skills.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy using work-energy theorem and transformations
skills.prompt: This topic covers thermodynamics for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should apply conservation of energy and entropy concepts to thermodynamic systems. Laws of thermodynamics: Zeroth (thermal equilibrium is transitive, defines temperature), First (ΔU = Q - W, conservation of energy), Second (entropy of isolated system never decreases; heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold), Third (absolute zero is unattainable). Internal energy: U depends on temperature for ideal gas; ΔU = nCvΔT. Work by gas: W = ∫PdV, area under P-V curve. Thermodynamic processes: isothermal (constant T, ΔU = 0, Q = W), isobaric (constant P, W = PΔV), isochoric (constant V, W = 0, Q = ΔU), adiabatic (Q = 0, ΔU = -W). PV diagrams: represent processes and cycles; area enclosed = net work for cycles. Heat engines: convert heat to work; efficiency η = W/QH = 1 - QC/QH; Carnot efficiency ηC = 1 - TC/TH (maximum possible). Refrigerators/heat pumps: move heat from cold to hot, require work input; COP = QC/W. Entropy: S = Q/T for reversible processes; ΔS ≥ 0 for isolated systems. Vocabulary: internal energy, entropy, enthalpy, isothermal, isobaric, isochoric, adiabatic, heat engine, Carnot cycle, refrigerator, coefficient of performance, PV diagram. Common misconceptions: heat and work are forms of energy stored in a system; 100% efficient heat engines are possible; entropy means disorder.

skills.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy including fluid dynamics principles
skills.prompt: This topic covers fluid mechanics for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should apply fluid statics and dynamics principles quantitatively. Fluid statics: pressure P = F/A, measured in pascals (Pa); hydrostatic pressure P = P0 + ρgh; pressure acts equally in all directions at a point. Pascal's principle: pressure change in enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished; hydraulic systems F1/A1 = F2/A2. Buoyancy: Archimedes' principle Fb = ρfluid × Vdisplaced × g; floating condition: object density < fluid density; fraction submerged = ρobject/ρfluid. Atmospheric pressure: ~101,325 Pa at sea level; measured by barometers and manometers. Fluid dynamics: ideal fluid assumptions (incompressible, non-viscous, steady, irrotational). Continuity equation: A1v1 = A2v2 (conservation of mass). Bernoulli's equation: P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant along streamline (conservation of energy). Applications: venturi meter, airplane lift (partial explanation), blood flow, water towers, siphons. Viscosity: real fluid resistance to flow; laminar vs. turbulent flow; Reynolds number determines flow regime. Vocabulary: pressure, pascal, buoyancy, Archimedes' principle, Pascal's principle, continuity, Bernoulli's equation, viscosity, laminar, turbulent, Reynolds number, streamline, manometer. Common misconceptions: lighter objects always float; Bernoulli's principle is the sole cause of lift; pressure pushes only downward.

skills.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy including kinetic molecular theory and thermodynamic processes
skills.prompt: This topic covers kinetic theory and PV diagrams for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should connect microscopic molecular behavior to macroscopic thermodynamic properties. Kinetic molecular theory (KMT): gas consists of many particles in random motion, particle size negligible compared to container, no intermolecular forces except during brief elastic collisions, kinetic energy proportional to temperature. Average translational KE: KE_avg = (3/2)kBT, where kB = 1.38 × 10^-23 J/K. Root-mean-square speed: vrms = √(3kBT/m) = √(3RT/M). Ideal gas law: PV = nRT = NkBT; connects P, V, T, and amount of gas. PV diagrams: graphical representation of thermodynamic processes; area under curve = work done by gas; clockwise cycle = heat engine (net work output), counterclockwise = refrigerator (net work input). Interpreting PV diagrams: isotherms (hyperbolas PV = constant), isobars (horizontal lines), isochors (vertical lines), adiabats (steeper than isotherms). Internal energy of ideal gas: U = (3/2)nRT for monatomic; U = (5/2)nRT for diatomic. First law applied to each process type using PV diagrams. Vocabulary: kinetic molecular theory, ideal gas, RMS speed, Boltzmann constant, PV diagram, isotherm, isobar, isochor, adiabat, degrees of freedom, equipartition theorem, internal energy. Common misconceptions: temperature measures total energy (it measures average KE); all gas molecules move at the same speed.

skills.skill: Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics
skills.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy including entropy and second law of thermodynamics
skills.prompt: This topic covers entropy and the second law of thermodynamics for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should understand entropy as a fundamental constraint on energy processes. Second law of thermodynamics: multiple equivalent statements: (Clausius) heat cannot spontaneously flow from cold to hot; (Kelvin-Planck) no heat engine can convert heat entirely to work in a cyclic process; (Entropy) the total entropy of an isolated system never decreases. Entropy: S is a state function measuring the number of accessible microstates; ΔS = Q/T for reversible processes; ΔS > 0 for irreversible (spontaneous) processes in isolated systems. Microstates and macrostates: entropy connects thermodynamic (macro) properties to statistical (micro) behavior; systems evolve toward macrostates with the most microstates (highest probability). Heat engines: efficiency η = 1 - QC/QH; Carnot engine (reversible) sets maximum efficiency ηCarnot = 1 - TC/TH. No real engine achieves Carnot efficiency due to irreversibilities (friction, turbulence, finite temperature gradients). Refrigerators and heat pumps: COP = QC/W (refrigerator) or QH/W (heat pump). Arrow of time: entropy gives time a direction; processes are irreversible because reverse would decrease total entropy. Free energy: ΔG = ΔH - TΔS; negative ΔG means spontaneous at constant T and P. Vocabulary: entropy, microstate, macrostate, reversible, irreversible, Carnot engine, second law, heat engine, efficiency, coefficient of performance, free energy, spontaneous. Common misconceptions: entropy means disorder; entropy can decrease in all systems; perfect efficiency is theoretically possible.

skills.teks_description: Analyze and construct series and parallel circuits using schematics
skills.prompt: This topic covers electric circuits for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.6(D)). Students should analyze complex DC circuits using Kirchhoff's laws and understand circuit behavior in depth. Circuit fundamentals: current I = dQ/dt, potential difference V, resistance R = ρL/A. Ohm's law: V = IR for ohmic materials. Kirchhoff's junction rule: ΣIin = ΣIout (charge conservation). Kirchhoff's loop rule: ΣΔV = 0 around any closed loop (energy conservation). Series resistors: Req = ΣRi, same current, voltages divide. Parallel resistors: 1/Req = Σ(1/Ri), same voltage, currents divide. RC circuits: charging capacitor V(t) = V0(1 - e^(-t/RC)), discharging V(t) = V0e^(-t/RC); time constant τ = RC. Power: P = IV = I²R = V²/R; energy dissipated E = Pt. Internal resistance: terminal voltage V = emf - Ir; maximum power transfer when R_load = r. Combination circuits: systematically reduce series and parallel combinations; for complex circuits, apply Kirchhoff's rules to write system of equations. Light bulb brightness: determined by power dissipated P = I²R = V²/R; changes when circuit configuration changes. Vocabulary: current, voltage, resistance, Kirchhoff's laws, series, parallel, equivalent resistance, RC circuit, time constant, EMF, internal resistance, power, energy. Common misconceptions: current is used up; batteries provide a constant current; adding resistors always increases total resistance.

skills.teks_description: Identify electric and magnetic forces and fields in everyday applications
skills.prompt: This topic covers magnetism for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.6(B)). Students should understand magnetic phenomena, magnetic materials, and the relationship between electricity and magnetism. Magnetic fields: produced by moving charges and current-carrying conductors; represented by field lines (north to south externally). Earth's magnetic field: protects from solar wind, used for navigation (geographic north ≈ magnetic south). Magnetic force on moving charge: F = qvB sin(θ), perpendicular to both v and B; charged particles move in circular paths in uniform fields (radius r = mv/qB, period T = 2πm/qB, independent of speed). Applications: mass spectrometer (separates ions by mass-to-charge ratio), cyclotron (particle accelerator), velocity selector (E and B fields crossed). Force on current-carrying wire: F = BIL sin(θ); parallel wires carrying current in same direction attract, opposite directions repel (F/L = μ0I1I2/(2πd)). Magnetic field sources: long wire B = μ0I/(2πr), center of circular loop B = μ0I/(2R), solenoid B = μ0nI. Ampere's law: ∮B·dl = μ0I_enclosed. Magnetic materials: ferromagnetic domains, hysteresis, electromagnets. Vocabulary: magnetic field, tesla, gauss, permeability, right-hand rule, Lorentz force, cyclotron, mass spectrometer, domain, hysteresis, Ampere's law. Common misconceptions: magnetic poles can exist independently; magnetic force can change a particle's speed; all metals respond to magnets.

skills.teks_description: Identify electromagnetic induction applications including inductance
skills.prompt: This topic covers inductance for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.6(B)). Students should understand self-inductance, mutual inductance, and energy stored in magnetic fields. Self-inductance: property of a coil that opposes changes in current; emf = -L(dI/dt), where L is inductance measured in henries (H). For solenoid: L = μ0n²Al (depends on geometry and core material). Mutual inductance: changing current in one coil induces emf in nearby coil; basis for transformers. RL circuits: when connected to voltage source, current grows exponentially I(t) = (V/R)(1 - e^(-Rt/L)); when disconnected, current decays I(t) = I0e^(-Rt/L); time constant τ = L/R. Energy stored in inductor: U = ½LI². Energy density in magnetic field: u = B²/(2μ0). LC circuits: current oscillates between inductor and capacitor; charge q(t) = Q0 cos(ωt) where ω = 1/√(LC); energy oscillates between electric (½CV²) and magnetic (½LI²) forms; analogous to mass-spring SHM. RLC circuits: damped oscillations due to resistance; underdamped, critically damped, overdamped behavior. Back-emf in motors: rotating coil in magnetic field generates emf opposing the driving voltage. Vocabulary: inductance, inductor, henry, self-inductance, mutual inductance, RL circuit, LC circuit, time constant, back-emf, energy density, oscillation. Common misconceptions: inductors store charge; inductors behave the same in DC steady state as in changing-current situations; LC oscillation continues forever.

skills.teks_description: Compare electromagnetic spectrum applications including the unification of electric and magnetic fields
skills.prompt: This topic covers Maxwell's equations for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.8(E)). Students should understand how Maxwell unified electricity and magnetism and predicted electromagnetic waves. Maxwell's four equations (conceptual understanding): (1) Gauss's law for electricity: electric flux through closed surface proportional to enclosed charge (charges create electric fields), (2) Gauss's law for magnetism: magnetic flux through closed surface is zero (no magnetic monopoles), (3) Faraday's law: changing magnetic flux induces electric field (basis for generators and transformers), (4) Ampere-Maxwell law: magnetic fields are produced by currents AND changing electric fields (displacement current). Maxwell's key insight: a changing electric field produces a magnetic field, just as a changing magnetic field produces an electric field; this symmetry allows self-propagating electromagnetic waves. Electromagnetic waves: oscillating E and B fields perpendicular to each other and to propagation direction; travel at c = 1/√(μ0ε0) = 3 × 10^8 m/s in vacuum; carry energy and momentum. EM spectrum: radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-ray, gamma (increasing frequency). Applications: radio transmission, microwave ovens, medical imaging, fiber optics, remote sensing. Vocabulary: Maxwell's equations, displacement current, electromagnetic wave, speed of light, electric flux, magnetic flux, electromagnetic spectrum, permittivity, permeability. Common misconceptions: EM waves need a medium; Maxwell's equations are four separate unrelated laws; radio waves and light are fundamentally different.

skills.teks_description: Compare electromagnetic spectrum applications including radio telescopes and x-rays
skills.prompt: This topic covers electromagnetic waves and the EM spectrum for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.8(E)). Students should compare electromagnetic spectrum regions and their applications. Electromagnetic wave properties: transverse waves with oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other and to direction of propagation; travel at c = 3 × 10^8 m/s in vacuum; require no medium; carry energy, momentum, and information. EM spectrum (increasing frequency/decreasing wavelength): radio waves (communication, astronomy), microwaves (cooking, radar, cell phones), infrared (thermal imaging, remote controls), visible light (human vision, ~400-700 nm), ultraviolet (sterilization, vitamin D, can cause skin damage), X-rays (medical imaging, airport security), gamma rays (cancer treatment, sterilization, nuclear processes). Wave relationships: c = fλ, E = hf = hc/λ. Energy and intensity: higher frequency = higher photon energy; intensity I = P/A; inverse-square law for point sources. Polarization: EM waves can be polarized (transverse nature); polarizing filters, Brewster's angle, applications (sunglasses, LCD screens, 3D movies). Radiation pressure: EM waves exert force F = P/c (absorbed) or 2P/c (reflected). Vocabulary: electromagnetic wave, electromagnetic spectrum, radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-ray, gamma ray, polarization, photon, wavelength, frequency, speed of light. Common misconceptions: microwaves are only for cooking; X-rays are fundamentally different from visible light; UV light is always harmful.

skills.teks_description: Describe image formation through plane mirror reflection and convex lens refraction
skills.prompt: This topic covers geometric and physical optics for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.8(G)). Students should analyze image formation and wave optics phenomena. Geometric optics (ray model): reflection (θi = θr), plane mirrors (virtual, upright, laterally inverted images). Curved mirrors: concave (converging, can form real or virtual images) and convex (diverging, always virtual images); mirror equation 1/f = 1/do + 1/di; magnification M = -di/do = hi/ho. Lenses: convex (converging) and concave (diverging); thin lens equation same form as mirror equation; sign conventions determine image characteristics (real/virtual, upright/inverted, magnified/reduced). Multiple lens systems: image from first lens becomes object for second. Physical optics: Young's double-slit experiment d sin(θ) = mλ (maxima), proves wave nature of light. Single-slit diffraction: a sin(θ) = mλ (minima); central maximum twice as wide as secondary maxima. Diffraction grating: d sin(θ) = mλ, produces sharp, well-separated maxima. Thin film interference: constructive and destructive depend on thickness, wavelength, and phase changes at boundaries. Resolution: Rayleigh criterion θmin = 1.22λ/D limits resolution of optical instruments. Vocabulary: focal length, real image, virtual image, magnification, ray diagram, diffraction, interference, diffraction grating, thin film, Rayleigh criterion, resolving power, dispersion. Common misconceptions: real images are visible without a screen; virtual images don't exist; bigger telescopes magnify more (they resolve more).

skills.teks_description: Explain superposition of quantum states related to light's wave-particle duality
skills.prompt: This topic covers quantum mechanics for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.9(C)). Students should understand wave-particle duality and fundamental quantum concepts. Wave-particle duality: light exhibits both wave properties (interference, diffraction) and particle properties (photoelectric effect, Compton scattering); matter also exhibits wave properties (de Broglie wavelength λ = h/mv, electron diffraction). Heisenberg uncertainty principle: ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2; cannot simultaneously know exact position and momentum; not a measurement limitation but fundamental nature of reality. Quantum superposition: particles exist in multiple states simultaneously until measured; Schrodinger's cat thought experiment illustrates the concept. Probability and wave functions: |ψ|² gives probability density of finding a particle at a position; particles do not have definite positions until measured. Quantum tunneling: particles can penetrate energy barriers they classically could not overcome; probability depends on barrier width, height, and particle energy; applications include scanning tunneling microscope, nuclear fusion in stars, tunnel diodes. Atomic orbitals: electrons described by quantum numbers (n, l, ml, ms), not definite orbits; electron cloud shapes (s, p, d, f). Vocabulary: wave-particle duality, uncertainty principle, superposition, wave function, probability density, quantum tunneling, de Broglie wavelength, quantum number, orbital, photon, complementarity. Common misconceptions: uncertainty principle is about measurement disturbing particles; electrons orbit like planets; quantum effects are observable at everyday scales.

skills.teks_description: Provide quantum phenomena examples including standard model applications
skills.prompt: This topic covers particle physics and the standard model for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.9(D)). Students should understand fundamental particles and forces. Standard model: framework classifying all known fundamental particles and three of four fundamental forces. Fermions (matter particles): quarks (up, down, charm, strange, top, bottom; have color charge, combine to form hadrons) and leptons (electron, muon, tau, and their neutrinos; do not experience strong force). Hadrons: baryons (three quarks, e.g., proton = uud, neutron = udd) and mesons (quark-antiquark pairs). Fundamental forces: strong nuclear (holds quarks together, mediated by gluons), electromagnetic (between charged particles, mediated by photons), weak nuclear (responsible for beta decay, mediated by W and Z bosons), gravity (mediated by hypothetical graviton, not included in standard model). Higgs boson: gives mass to fundamental particles via Higgs mechanism; discovered 2012 at CERN. Conservation laws: charge, baryon number, lepton number, energy-momentum in particle reactions. Matter-antimatter: each particle has an antiparticle with opposite charge; annihilation produces photons (E = 2mc²). Key experiments: particle accelerators, cosmic ray detection, neutrino observatories. Vocabulary: quark, lepton, baryon, meson, hadron, boson, fermion, gluon, photon, W/Z boson, Higgs boson, antiparticle, annihilation, standard model, conservation law. Common misconceptions: atoms are the smallest particles; protons and neutrons are fundamental; antimatter is science fiction.

skills.teks_description: Explain advanced quantum concepts and relativistic effects on energy and mass
skills.prompt: This topic covers special relativity for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.9(C)). Students should understand Einstein's special relativity and its consequences for energy and mass. Postulates of special relativity: (1) the laws of physics are the same in all inertial reference frames, (2) the speed of light c is the same for all observers regardless of relative motion. Consequences: time dilation (Δt = γΔt0, moving clocks run slower; γ = 1/√(1 - v²/c²)), length contraction (L = L0/γ, moving objects are shorter in direction of motion), relativity of simultaneity (events simultaneous in one frame may not be in another). Mass-energy equivalence: E = mc² (rest energy), E = γmc² (total energy), KE = (γ - 1)mc². Relativistic momentum: p = γmv; as v → c, momentum approaches infinity (cannot accelerate mass to c). Speed of light: ultimate speed limit; requires infinite energy to accelerate massive particle to c. Evidence: muon decay in atmosphere (time dilation), particle accelerator experiments (mass increase), GPS satellite corrections, atomic clocks on aircraft. Key concepts: proper time, proper length, reference frames, Lorentz factor, spacetime, four-vectors. Vocabulary: special relativity, time dilation, length contraction, Lorentz factor, rest mass, rest energy, relativistic momentum, spacetime, proper time, proper length, inertial frame, simultaneity. Common misconceptions: nothing can travel faster than light (information/mass cannot, but expansion of space can); relativistic effects only matter near light speed (GPS needs corrections); E = mc² means mass converts to energy.
